statusLine: EU fuel security is under strain with 17 of 27 countries showing critical reserves in at least one fuel type, despite overall averages remaining above mandatory minimums.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel security faces significant pressure with widespread critical shortages across multiple countries. Romania shows the most severe situation with all three fuel types at critical levels—petrol at just 33.2 days, diesel at 32.4 days, and jet fuel at 76.4 days. Poland, Luxembourg, and Germany also face critical shortages across all fuel categories, with Poland's jet fuel reserves particularly concerning at only 21.3 days.

While EU-wide averages remain above the mandatory 90-day minimum (petrol 98.9 days, diesel 98 days, jet fuel 91.9 days), the distribution is highly uneven. A total of 17 of 27 countries show critical status in at least one fuel type, with diesel reserves under the most pressure—15 countries report critical diesel stocks. Eastern European nations appear particularly vulnerable, with Romania, Poland, Lithuania, and Slovakia all showing multiple critical categories.

The fuel price landscape reflects ongoing market tensions, with Brent crude at $92.51 per barrel despite a recent 6.06% decline. Consumer prices vary dramatically across the bloc, from Malta's €1.34 per liter for petrol to the Netherlands' €2.316 per liter. This 73% price differential highlights the fragmented nature of EU fuel markets during periods of supply stress.
keyPoints: 17 of 27 EU countries report critical fuel reserves in at least one category, despite overall averages meeting mandatory minimums
Romania faces the most severe shortages with all fuel types at critical levels, including petrol at just 33.2 days
Diesel supplies are under the greatest pressure EU-wide, with 15 countries reporting critical diesel stocks below 90 days
Eastern European countries show particular vulnerability, with Poland, Romania, and Lithuania among the most affected
Fuel prices vary dramatically across the EU, with a 73% spread between cheapest (Malta €1.34/L) and most expensive (Netherlands €2.316/L) petrol prices
